text:
The Senescence-Associated-Secretory Phenotype constituting HIF-1 α activation independent of micronuclei induction and associated cGAS-mediated interferon response

Deng Lin 1,8 ,  Celestine Ho 2,3,8 ,  Remigio Picone 4,5,8 ,  Fieda Abderazzaq 6 ,  Nicole Flanagan 6 Chuan Low 2,7 , Selwin K. Wu 4,5 *

1 Institute of Molecular Physiology, Shenzhen Bay Laboratory, Shenzhen, China

2 Mechanobiology Institute, National University of Singapore, Singapore

3 Department of Biomedical Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore

4 Department of Cell Biology, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA

5 Department of Pediatric Oncology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Boston, MA, USA

6 Center  for  Cancer  Computational  Biology,  Department  of  Biostatistics  and  Computational Biology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Boston, MA, USA

7 Department  of  Biological  Sciences  and  NUS  College,  National  University  of  Singapore, Singapore

8 Equal contributions

* Correspondence: selwin_wu@mail.dfci.harvard.edu, Present address: Mechanobiology Institute, National University of Singapore, Singapore

ABSTRACT

The Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype (SASP), characterized by the upregulation of inflammatory  cytokines,  is  triggered  during  senescence  by  anti-proliferation  stresses,  such  as replicative  exhaustion  and  centrosome  amplification.  Previously,  we  have  discovered  that centrosome amplification activates a non-canonical SASP that is dominated by HIF-1 α -induced inflammatory cytokines instead of the NF κ b activation in canonical SASP. HIF-1 α is  not  only activated  in  centrosome  amplification,  oncogene,  and  gamma-irradiation-induced  senescence 1 but  also  induced  in  replicative  exhaustion-induced  senescence.  Another  major  consequence  of centrosome  amplification  is  micronuclei  generation  from  chromosome  missegregation.  The micronuclei  could  have  triggered  SASP  by  activating  cGAS/Sting  innate  immune  response 2 . However, it has been shown that micronuclei from chromosome missegregation fail to activate cGAS/Sting-mediated  innate  immune  response 3-5 .  Indeed,  here  we  further  demonstrate  that moderate chromosome missegregation by centrosome amplification or mitotic kinase monopolar

is , Boon spindle  1  inhibition,  which  is  known  to  generate  micronuclei,  fails  to  induce  senescence  and cGAS-mediated  interferon  response.  In  summary,  we  provide  evidence  that  the  SenescenceAssociated-Secretory Phenotype constitutes hypoxia-inducible factor-alpha activation, independent of chromosome missegregation triggering cGAS-mediated innate immune interferon response.

INTRODUCTION

Cellular  senescence  is  characterized  by  the  upregulation  of  pro-inflammatory  cytokines,  a phenomenon termed the Senescence-Associated-Secretory Phenotype  (SASP) 6 . Cellular senescence  is  triggered  by  many  stresses 7 ,  including  replicative  exhaustion 8 and  centrosome amplification 1 .  Here,  we  will  focus  on  centrosome  amplification  and  replicative  exhaustioninduced  senescence.  Centrosomes  are  microtubule  organizing  centers,  which  in  turn  support cellular  processes  such  as  cell  division  and  adhesion 9 .  In  most  normal  cells,  centrosomes  are duplicated once during each cell division such that G1 phase cells typically have one centrosome and mitotic cells have two. By contrast, senescent and cancer cells commonly exhibit increased numbers of centrosomes, termed centrosome amplification. Notably, centrosome amplification can  induce  proliferation  arrest  by  PIDDosome  multiprotein  complex 10 and  Hippo  pathwaymediated  mechanisms.  Centrosome  amplification  also  stimulates  reactive  oxygen  species production 1 to upregulate the expression of SASP factors. Of note, these secreted SASP factors induce cell-cell contact disruption 11 , tumor growth 12 , and invasion 1 .

During  cell  division,  centrosome  amplification  promotes  chromosome  missegregation  that generates  micronuclei 13 .  Micronuclei  generated  in  centrosome  amplification  cells  could  have triggered SASP, by activating cGAS/Sting 14 . Cytosolic DNA  sensor cyclic guanosine monophosphate  (GMP)-AMP  synthase  (cGAS)  functions  as  a  cytosolic  DNA  sensor  that activates the innate immune interferon response when bound to dsDNA in the cytosol 15 . In brief, activated  cGAS,  when  bound  to  dsDNA,  converts  ATP  and  guanosine  triphosphate  (GTP)  to 2 ′ 3 ′ -cyclic  GMP-AMP  (cGAMP).  cGAMP  binds  and  activates  stimulator  of  interferon  genes (STING). Thus, resulting in TANK-binding kinase 1 kinase-dependent phosphorylation, nuclear translocation  of  interferon  regulatory  factor  3,  and  transcriptional  activation  of  interferon- stimulated  genes 16 .  Thus,  missegregated  chromosomes  forming  micronuclei  with  defective nuclear envelope, that are exposed to the cytoplasm, have been reported to activate cGAS/Sting 2 .

However,  recent  reports  indicate  that  micronuclei  from  chromosome  missegregation  fail  to activate  cGAS/Sting  mediated  innate  immune  response 3-5 . cGAS  localization to nuclear envelope-ruptured  micronuclei  during  interphase  was  reported  to  be  rare 3-5 ,  with  cGAS  more often  binding  to  micronuclei  during  mitosis  and  remaining  on  cytosolic  chromatin.  Notably, cGAS accumulation during mitosis did not activate STING or trigger the interferon response in the  subsequent  interphase 3, 5 .  Amongst  the  cellular  senescence  triggers,  gamma-irradiation activated STING independently of micronuclei formation and cGAS localization to micronuclei, suggesting  that  micronuclei  are  not  the  trigger  for  cGAS/STING  pathway  activation 5 .  Indeed, STING-mediated interferon response after irradiation is linked to cytosolic mitochondria DNA release instead 3 . The presence of histones in micronuclei was found to be a potential inhibitory factor,  preventing  cGAS  activation 5 .  Consistent  with  reports  that  micronuclei  do  not  activate cGAS,  here  we  demonstrate  that  moderate  chromosome  missegregation  induction  by  mitotic kinase  monopolar  spindle  1  (MPS1)  inhibition  or  centrosome  amplification,  which  generates micronuclei 5, 13 fails to induce senescence and interferon response.

Besides  the  implications  of  micronuclei  and  cGAS/Sting  signaling  in  senescence,  we  have discovered  that  hypoxia  signaling  is  activated  in  centrosome  amplification,  oncogene,  and gamma-irradiation-induced senescence 1 .  HIF  are  key  transcription  factors that  regulate  oxygen sensing and mediate the hypoxic response 17 . Activation of HIF triggers the transcription of genes crucially involved in cancer 18 and immunity 19 , including cytokines such as IL8, IL6, ANGPTL4, VEGF, and PDGF, which are commonly observed in SASP 6, 20-22 .

Cellular senescence triggers such as constitutively active Ras overexpression, which promotes invasion,  metastasis,  and  cell  extrusion 23,  24  25 ,  also  activate  HIF-1 α 1,  26,  27 .  Similarly,  gammairradiation,  which  promotes  inflammation and  senescence,  triggers  HIF  activation 1,  28 .  Besides centrosome amplification,  oncogene,  and  gamma-irradiation-induced  senescence,  we  here  find that senescence is induced by replicative exhaustion. Replicative exhaustion-induced senescence is often partly a result of telomeric dysfunction (telomere uncapping) resulting from repeated cell division 29 .  Although  evidence  suggests  that  hypoxia  and  HIF  activation  can  prevent  or  delay cells from entering senescence 30 . Nevertheless, the HIF activity of cells once they have entered replicative exhaustion-induced senescence remains unknown. Here, applying Gene Set Enrichment  Analysis  (GSEA) 31 on  whole-transcriptome  datasets,  we  reveal  hypoxia  induction and response in replicative exhausted senescent cells but not in quiescence cells.

In  summary,  we  demonstrate  that the  Senescence-Associated-Secretory  Phenotype  induced by centrosome amplification and replicative  exhaustion constitutes  hypoxia-inducible  factor-alpha pathway activation, independent of chromosome missegregation triggering the cGAS-mediated inteferon response.

MATERIALS & METHODS

Cell culture

All  source  cell  culture  was  maintained  in  media  containing  plasmocin  (1:5000  dilution, Invivogen)  to  prevent  mycoplasma  contamination.  Culture  plates  were  briefly  coated  with 40 μ g/ml of fibronectin (Sigma) and glass coverslips were incubated in 40 μ g/ml fibronectin for at least 2 hours. Human mammary epithelial MCF10A cells were maintained at 37 0 C with 5% CO2 atmosphere and cultured as previously described 14 . In brief, MCF10A cells were cultured in phenol red-free DMEM/F12 (Invitrogen) supplemented with 5% horse serum (Sigma), 20ng/ml epidermal growth factor (EGF; Sigma), 10mg/ml insulin (Invitrogen), 100 mg/ml hydrocortisone (Sigma),  1ng/ml  cholera  toxin  (Sigma)  and  100  IU/ml  penicillin  and  100 μ g/ml  streptomycin (Invitrogen).  WI38  lung  fibroblast  cells,  obtained  from  the  ATCC,  were  cultured  in  ATCCformulated Eagle's Minimum Essential Medium containing 10% FBS.

To conditionally overexpress PLK4, lentiviral vectors pLenti-CMV-TetR-Blast (17492, Addgene)  and  pLenti-CMV/TO-Neo-Dest  (17292,  Addgene)  were  used.  Wild-type  PLK4  and PLK4  1-608  cDNAs  (control  608)  were  cloned  using  the  Gateway  system  into  the  pLentiCMV/TO-Neo-Dest vector. W138 lung fibroblasts were coinfected with a lentivirus containing the  TetR  and  with  the  lentivirus  containing  the  wild-type  PLK4  or  control  608  open  reading frames.

Induction of centrosome amplification and senescence

At day 0, control 608 and PLK4 cells were gently dissociated into single cells, then seeded at low density into media with 2 μ g/ml of doxycycline such that they were ~80% confluent on day 5. After ~48 hours of doxycycline incubation, the culture media with doxycycline was replaced. On day 5,  cells  are  gently  dissociated  into  single  cells  before  reseeding  into  6  well  plates  to ~80%  confluent  on  day  7.  Because  control  608  cells  grow  faster  than  PLK4  induced  cells, control 608 cells are seeded at ~2.5 times lower density than PLK4 induced cells. Cells at ~80% confluency are analysed either at day 7 or day 8. Media is left unchanged for ~3 days before analysis.

Generation of tetraploids and 'evolved' tetraploids with normal centrosome numbers

To  generate  G1  arrested  tetraploids,  15cm  dishes  were  seeded  with  6  million  exponentially growing RPE1-FUCCI cells, such that they were ∼ 65% confluent the following day. Day 2: 4 μ M Dihydrocytochalasin B (DCB) was added to each 15 cm dish for 16 hr. Day 3: DCB-treated cells were washed 5 times for 5 minutes, incubated in medium containing 2.5 μ g/ml Hoechst dye for 1 hr, then trypsinized in 0.05% trypsin. G1 diploids (2C DNA content; mCherry+, GFP-) and G1  tetraploids  (4C  DNA  content;  mCherry+,  GFP-)  were  isolated  by  FACS  sorting.  FACSsorted cells were seeded on 6 well plates coated with 40 μ g/ml fibronectin.

Evolved tetraploid RPE-1 cells,  kindly  provided by  Huadi  Zhang,  with the  normal number  of centrosomes were derived as described 13 .  Briefly, tetraploid RPE-1 cells were treated for 16 hr with  0.2 μ m  cytochalasin  D,  and  FACS-sorted  by  DNA  content  using  Hoechst  staining (Molecular Probes). The peak of cells with a DNA content of 8C were isolated and cultured for ∼ 1  week  before  a  second  iteration  of  the  FACS  sorting  to  re-isolate  8C  cells.  A  total  of  5 purifying cell sortings for ∼ 6 weeks were required to generate a pure population of proliferating tetraploid cells with normal centrosome numbers.

CRISPR Knockouts and Lentiviral Transduction

The previously  described 32 HIF-1 α pLenti-CRISPRv2  was  a  gift  from  William  G.  Kaelin,  Jr. (Dana-Farber  Cancer  Institute,  Boston,  USA).  The  sgRNA  sequence  targeting  HIF-1 α is  as follow: '5'- CACCGTGTGAGTTCGCATCTTGATA -'3'. Clonal selection for single-cell clones after puromycin selection were used for the experiments.

For lentivirus production, 6 μ g lentiviral constructs, 3 μ g psPAX2 (Addgene), and 3 μ g pMD2.G (Addgene)  were transfected  into  293T  cells  at  80%  confluency  in  a 10-cm  dish  with 8  ml  of media.  20 μ l  of  lipofectamine  3000  (Life  Technologies)  was  used  and  transfection  was performed  according  to  manufacturer's  instructions.  Virus  was  harvested  at  48  hours  and  72 hours  post-infection  and  filtered  with  a  0.2 μ m  filter 33,  34 .  MCF10A  cells  were  infected  for 12 hours,  with  virus  containing  the  genes  of  interest  in  the  presence  of  10 μ g/ml  polybrene, washed thoroughly, and allowed to recover for 24 hours before any selection.

Antibodies and drugs

Primary antibodies used in this study were: rabbit monoclonal antibodies against cGAS (1:1000 for  western  blotting;  Cell  Signaling  Technologies  catalog  number  15102S,  clone  number D1D3G),  mouse  monoclonal  antibodies  against  HIF-1 α (1:50-1:100  for  immunofluorescence; Abcam catalog number ab16066)

Doxycycline  (Sigma  Aldrich  catalog  number  D3072)  was  used  at  2  mg/ml.  TNF α (Roche Diagnostics catalog number 11371843001) was used at 20ng/ml for 2 hours. The following doses of  inhibitors  were  used:  0.1  and  0.2 μ M  Reversine  (Cayman  Chemical)  4 μ M  of  dihydro cytochalasin B (DCB; Sigma).

Immunofluorescence and live-cell microscopy

Cells were fixed at 4°C with 4% paraformaldehyde in cytoskeleton stabilization buffer (10mM PIPES at pH 6.8, 100mM KCl, 300mM sucrose, 2mM EGTA, and 2mM MgCl2) on ice for 25 min  and  subsequently  permeabilized  with  0.2%  Triton-X  in  PBS  for  10  minutes  at  room temperature 33,  35-40 .  Fixed  cells  were  blocked  with 5% BSA in TBS overnight at 4°C. Primary antibodies were incubated overnight at 4°C for HIF-1 α and 45 minutes at room temperature for p65/RELA. For all immunostaining, secondary Alexa Fluor antibodies were incubated at room temperature for an hour.

For  live  cell  imaging,  time-lapse  images  were  collected  with  a  Nikon  Ti  inverted  microscope with  20x  wide-field  optics  equipped  with  a  perfect  focus  system,  a  Hamamatsu  ORCA  ER

cooled  CCD camera controlled  with  Nikon  NIS-Element  software,  and  an  OkoLab  37°C,  5% CO2 microscope chamber.

Media  Swap  Motility  Assay Cells  were  plated  at  0.5-1x10 6 per  well  in  6  well  plates  and allowed  to  attach  for  24h.  Then,  cells  were  starved  in  serum  free  medium  for  two  hours  and incubated in 0.5% horse serum media for another 12 hours. Conditioned medium from cells with or without centrosome amplification were collected and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 minutes to eliminate any detached cells or debris. Conditioned medium was then added to cells and the effect of the swapped conditioned medium on the cellular motility was imaged with bright field microscopy (Nikon Ti).

NextSeq500 RNA Library Preparation and Sequencing

RNA quantity was determined on the Qubit using the Qubit RNA Assay Kit (Life Tech) and RNA quality was determined on the Bioanalyzer using the RNA Pico Kit (Agilent).  Using the NEB Next Ultra RNA Library Prep Kit for Illumina (NEB), which selects for poly(A) mRNA, 100ng of total RNA was used for cDNA library construction, according to the manufacturer's protocol.  Following  library  construction,  DNA  quantity  was  determined using  the  Qubit  High Sensitivity  DNA  Kit  (Life  Technologies),  and  library  insert  size  was  determined  using  the Bioanalyzer  High  Sensitivity  Chip  Kit  (Agilent).  Finally,  qPCR  was  performed  using  the Universal  Library  Quantification  Kit  for  Illumina  (Kapa  Biosystems)  and  run  on  the  7900HT Fast qPCR machine (ABI) for quantification and normalization of libraries.  Libraries that passed quality control were diluted to 2nM DNA content using sterile water and then sequenced on the NextSeq500  (Illumina)  at  a  final  concentration  of  12pM,  according  to  the  manufacturer's instructions.

RNA Sequencing Analysis

Sequencing reads were aligned to the reference genome using the RNA-specific STAR aligner using  default  parameters 41 .  The  quality  of  sequencing  runs  and  alignments  was  assessed  by RNA-SeQC 42 .  The  featureCounts  tool  was  used  to  count  sequencing  reads  mapped  to  the reference  genome  at  the  gene  level 43 .   The  counts  were  subsequently  normalized  between samples using DESeq2 to control for experimental variation 44 .  Differential expression analysis was performed between the desired experimental contrasts using DESeq2.

Gene Set Enrichment Analysis

We  used  GSEA  v3.0  to  examine  the  association  between  gene  sets  and  gene  expression. Normalised read counts from DESeq2 analysis were used for GSEA. Genes with a 0 read count in either control or experimental group were excluded. GSEA was performed using gene sets as permutation type, 1,000 permutations and log2 ratio of classes as metric for ranking genes. To construct a HIFα signature upregulated gene set, we performed RNA sequencing of MCF10A cells  after  overnight  treatment  with  1 μ m of DMOG, with DMSO-treated cells as control. We included  genes  that  were  upregulated  by  DMOG  in  both  DESeq  and  DESeq2  analysis (p(adjusted)<0.05,  fold  change  >  4,  Supplementary  Table  1).  To  construct  a  DNA-damage senescence gene set we performed RNA sequencing of MCF10A cells 7 days after exposure to 12Gy  of γ -irradiation.  We  obtained  published  data  sets  to  construct  both  upregulated  and downregulated core senescence gene sets 45 .

Quantitative  RT-PCR Total  RNA  was  isolated  using  the  RNAeasy  Mini  Kit  (Qiagen),  and cDNA was synthesised using SuperScript III (Invitrogen) according to manufacturer instructions. Quantitative RT-qPCR was performed in triplicate using SYBR Green (Life Technologies) on a ViiA  7  Real-Time  PCR  machine  (Thermo  Fisher  Scientific)  with  GAPDH  as an  internal normalisation control. Sequences of qPCR primers for cGAS are as follows: Forward primer: 5'-gggagccctgctgtaacacttcttat-3', Reverse primer: 5'-cctttgcatgcttgggtacaaggt-3'

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

The hallmark hypoxia genes are induced in cellular senescence

The transcriptome datasets used here were validated by analysis of their senescence expression pattern. Many senescence-associated markers result from altered transcription, but the senescent phenotype  is  variable.  This  variability  might  be  due  to  tissue  and  cell-type  differences, differences in the senescence trigger 46 , and the dynamic nature of gene expression in senescent cells 47-49 . Despite variabilities in the senescent phenotype, a core transcriptome signature of a list of  upregulated  and  downregulated  genes  has  recently  been  identified  as  a  useful  marker  to consistently  identify  senescent  cells 45 .  Thus,  we  employ  this  universal  senescent  gene  set  to confirm the senescent identity of cells that underwent gamma-irradiation, replicative exhaustion and constitutive  Ras  oncogene-induced  senescence.  MCF10A  cells  were  exposed  to  12  Gy  of gamma-irradiation,  whereas  the  replicative  exhaustion  and  constitutive  active  Ras-induced IMR90 cells transcriptome were obtained from published datasets 50 investigating the senescenceassociated secretory phenotype. Seven days post-irradiation, the transcriptomes of control cells and  gamma-irradiated  cells  were  compared.  GSEA  determines  the  transcriptional  program activated  by  identifying  coordinated  changes  in  a  defined  set  of  gene  expressions 31 .  Gene  set enrichment  analysis  revealed  a  strong  association  between  gamma-irradiation  (Figure  1a,  b), replicative exhaustion (Figure 1c,d), and constitutive-active Ras oncogene (Figure 1e, f) induced genes with both upregulated and downregulated senescence gene sets.

We  then  used  GSEA  to  identify  transcriptional  signatures  that  are  commonly  activated  in gamma-irradiation, replicative exhaustion, and constitutive-active Ras oncogene-induced senescence.  We  employ  the  hallmark  gene  sets  from  the  Molecular  Signatures  Database (MSigDB) 31, 51, 52 . Hallmark gene sets summarize and represent specific, well-defined biological states  or  processes  and  display  coherent  expression.  These  gene  sets  were  generated  by  a computational  methodology  based  on  identifying  gene  set  overlaps  and  retaining  genes  that display coordinate expression, thus reducing data noise and redundancy. Strikingly, the hypoxia hallmark was identified as a common expression signature profile from our GSEA of replicative exhausted,  gamma-irradiated,  and  Ras-induced  cells  (Figure  1g,h).  The  hallmark  hypoxia signature  was  ranked  3 rd and  8 th in  replicative  and  Ras-induced  senescence,  respectively.  The hallmark hypoxia signature was ranked 17 th in  our  irradiated  cells,  ahead  of  the  p53  hallmark response- which is a key feature of cellular senescence. Commonly induced hallmark gene sets also include the inflammatory response, NF κ B activation, and IL2/STAT5 signaling (Figure 1h). These immune-associated expression programs were known to be commonly induced in cellular senescence 53 . The top 30 genes in the hallmark hypoxia GSEA leading-edge subset from gammairradiated,  replicative  exhausted  and  constitutive  active  Ras-induced  includes  well-validated HIF-1 α targets such as ANGPTL4, PDGFB and VEGFA (Fig 1i, j, k).

HIF-1 α target  genes  are  induced  in  SASP  and  replicative  exhaustion-induced  senescence but not in cellular quiescence

HIF-1 α is a critical sensor of low oxygen levels 54,  55 .  In normoxia, prolyl hydroxylases (PHDs) hydroxylate  HIF-1 α ,  triggering  von  Hippel-Lindau-mediated  ubiquitination  and  proteasomal degradation ￼ . Conversely, hypoxia inhibits PHDs and stabilizes HIF-1 α , which then translocate into the nucleus and dimerizes with constitutively expressed HIF-1 β , activating the HIF complex and triggering the transcription of genes promoting inflammation and cancer 56 .

A  gene-expression  signature  of  HIFα activation  is  derived  from  transcriptomic  analysis  of MCF10A normal breast epithelial cells treated with Dimethyloxalylglycine, N(Methoxyoxoacetyl)-glycine methyl ester (DMOG) 1 . DMOG  promotes the steady-state accumulation  of  HIF-1 α because  it  inhibits  the  EGLN/PHD  prolyl  hydroxylases,  which  mark HIF-1 α for  proteasomal  degradation 57 .  Strikingly,  expression  of  SASP  factors  in  general  is induced in DMOG-treated cells (Figure 2a,b). Common SASP factors such as IGFBP3, IL8, and VEGF are among the top 20 genes in the SASP GSEA leading-edge subset (Figure 2b). This custom  HIF-1 α activation  gene  set  is  significantly  enriched  in  replicative  exhaustion-induced senescent cells (Fig 2c,d). By contrast, our analysis of two previously published datasets for the induction  of  cellular  quiescence  in  IMR90  fibroblasts 58 (Figure  2e,f)  and  LFS  MDAH041 fibroblast  cells 8 (Figure  2g,h)  revealed  that  both  the  hallmark  hypoxia  and  HIF-1 α activation gene sets were either not induced (Figure 3e,f) or significantly suppressed (Figure 2g,h). Thus, suggesting that the induction of HIF-1 α genes is specific to the induction of cellular senescence but not quiescence.

Centrosome amplification induces paracrine MCF10A cell protrusions and senescence in WI38 fibroblasts

Next, we analyze the consequence of centrosome amplification-induced senescence. Centrosome amplification  was  induced  in  cells  by  overexpressing  Polo-like  kinase  4  (PLK4),  the  master regulatory kinase for centrosome duplication 59, 60 . PLK4 was transiently induced, and subsequent analysis was performed at time points where increased PLK4 mRNA expression was no longer detectable 61 .  In  most experiments described below, cells with the transient overexpression of a truncated  PLK4  mutant  (Control  608)  with  kinase  activity,  which  did  not  localize  to  the centrosomes 62 , were used as the negative control.

We previously found that centrosome amplification can induce paracrine invasion by activating a SASP 1 .  The  induction  of  paracrine  motility  by  secreted  factors  is  a  hallmark  of  SASP 6 .  To visualize whether cells with centrosome amplification can induce dynamic protrusions of normal cells in a paracrine manner, we swapped the conditioned medium between MCF10A cells with centrosome  amplification  and  the  conditioned  medium  from  control  cells  without  centrosome amplification. As expected, the control conditioned medium did not alter the protrusion velocity of  cells  with  centrosome  amplification  (Fig.3a,b,d  and  Supplementary  Video  1).  By  contrast, conditioned  media  from  cells  with  centrosome  amplification  induced  an  approximately  2-fold increase  in  the  velocity  of  leading  edge  movement  in  cells  lacking  centrosome  amplification (Fig.3a,c,d and Supplementary Video 2).

Besides Retinal Pigment Epithelial-1 (RPE-1) and MCF-10A epithelial cells entering senescence when induced  with  extra  centrosomes  seven  days  post-induction 1 ,  WI38  lung  fibroblasts  also exhibit  senescence-associated  beta-galactosidase  activity  (~60% positive cells,  Fig.  3e,f)  when induced  with  extra  centrosomes  by  transient  PLK4  expression.  Additionally,  senescenceassociated heterochromatin foci were observed after the induction of centrosome amplification by transient PLK4 expression in WI38 cells(Fig. 3g,h). As senescence-associated heterochromatin foci is a cell-type specific phenotype 63 , senescence-associated heterochromatin foci are not observed in MCF10A or RPE-1 cells (data not shown).

Centrosome amplification induced a non-canonical SASP with an inflammatory response dominated by HIF-1 α instead of the classical NF κ B

Although  centrosome  amplification  generated  many  cellular  features  and  gene  expression characteristics  of  senescence,  several  aspects  of  the  response  were  distinct  from  senescence induced by canonical triggers 1 . First, the SASP induced by centrosome amplification was distinct from  the  SASP  induced  by  conventional  triggers  dominated  by  NFκ B  activation.    NFκ B  is generally  thought  to  be  the  primary  determinant  of  the  SASP  secretome 64 ,  and  DNA  damage signaling  was  recently  shown  to  activate  NFκ B through the transcription  factor  GATA4 64,  65 . However,  centrosome  amplification  induction  of  proinflammatory  cytokines  was  modest  by comparison  with  the  SASP  induced  by γ -irradiation  or  oncogene  activation  (Fig  4a) 50,  64 . Moreover, there is no detectable GATA4 mRNA in MCF10A cells, with or without centrosome amplification (Fig 4a).

However,  by  GSEA,  we  did  observe  enrichment  for  NFκ B-regulated  genes  in  the  samples where centrosome amplification was induced by PLK4 transient overexpression (Fig 4b). But this enrichment for NFκ B-regulated genes was abolished by CRISPR-mediated gene disruption of HIF-1 α (Fig 4c), suggesting that the shared gene targets of HIF-1 α with NFκ B is contributing to  the  enrichment of NFκ B-regulated genes 19 .  Additionally,  we  did not observe a significant enrichment  for  NFκ B-regulated  genes  in  the  samples  where  centrosome  amplification  was induced by cytokinesis failure (Fig 4b). Strikingly, quantitative imaging indicated that centrosome amplification in WI38 lung fibroblasts did not result in any detectable increase in the nuclear  localization  of  NFκ B  (Fig.  4d,e).    Thus,  centrosome  amplification  induces  a  noncanonical SASP that is dominated by HIF-1 α activation.

Centrosome  amplification  can  induce  SASP  independent  of  moderate  chromosome missegregation and micronuclei activation of cGAS

Second, the effects of centrosome amplification are distinct from those of aneuploidy. Although aneuploidy can induce cell cycle arrest and senescence, it is established that this occurs at high levels of aneuploidy that disrupt DNA replication and cause DNA damage 66, 67 .  Accordingly, we confirm that a low level of aneuploidy (MPS1 inhibition by reversine), a comparable level to that induced by centrosome amplification 61 , does not trigger proliferation arrest (Fig 5a,b) or induce HIF-1 α (Fig 5c,d).

Lastly, senescence from various conventional triggers, for example, 12 Gy gamma-irradiation of MCF10A (Fig 5e,f), triggers an interferon response, possibly due to cytosolic mitochondria DNA release  activating  STING 3 .  However,  in  the  SASP  that  occurs  after  centrosome  amplification, interferon  response  genes  are  suppressed,  not  induced  (Fig  5g,h).  A  major  consequence  of centrosome amplification is chromosome missegregation generation of micronuclei. Our data is consistent  with  reports  indicating  that  cGAS-mediated  interferon  response  is  not  activated  by micronuclei 3-5 .  Moreover,  RPE-1  cells,  which  manifest  an  upregulation  of  secreted  protein expression after senescence induction by various triggers 66 , including centrosome amplification 1 , lack detectable expression of cGAS (Fig 5i,j).  Thus, indicating that cGAS is not essential for cellular  senescence  in  RPE-1  cells.  In  conclusion,  we  provide  evidence  that  the  SenescenceAssociated-Secretory Phenotype constituting HIF-1 α activation  is independent of chromosome missegregation triggering the cGAS-mediated innate immune interferon response.

FIGURES

Figure 1. The hallmark hypoxia genes are induced in cellular senescence.

(a,b,c,d,e,f)  GSEA  shows  that  the  senescence  up-regulated  (a,c,e)  and  down-regulated  (b,d,f) gene sets are induced in the 12 Gy γ -irradiation (a,b), replicative exhaustion (c,d), and RasG12Vinduced cells (e,f).

- (g) GSEA shows that the hallmark hypoxia gene set is induced in replicative exhaustion-induced senescence.
- (h)  Eight  hallmark  expression  signatures  are  commonly  upregulated  in  12  Gy γ -irradiation, replicative  exhausted, and Ras  G12V-induced  senescent  cells.  Venn diagram  for  the  indicated comparisons showing the overlap of the hallmark expression signatures from all experimental conditions.  The  bottom panel  is a table  of  the  hallmark  expression  signatures  with  at  least  an FDR  of  q  <  0.05  shared  between  Ras  &  replicative-exhaustion,  Ras  &  irradiation,  and replicative-exhaustion & irradiation-induced senescence, respectively.
- (i,j,k) Heatmaps showing the leading-edge enrichment of the top hallmark hypoxia 30 genes in (i) 12 Gy γ -irradiation, (j) replicative exhaustion, and (k) RasG12V-induced cells.

Figure 2. Activation of HIF target genes triggers SASP factors expression, and replicative exhaustion activates HIF target genes but not in quiescent cells.

- (a) GSEA showing that the SASP factors gene set is enriched in DMOG-treated cells.
- (b) Heatmap showing the leading-edge enrichment of the top 20 SASP factors in DMOG-treated MCF10A cells.
- (c) GSEA showing that the custom HIFα signature up is enriched in replicative exhausted cells.
- (d) Heatmap  showing  the  leading-edge enrichment of the  top  custom  HIFα signature upregulated 30 genes in replicative exhausted cells.

(e,f,g,h)  GSEA  showing  that  the  (e,g)  hallmark  hypoxia  and  (f,h)  custom  HIFα signature  up gene sets are suppressed in (e,f) IMR90 58 and (g,h) LFS MDAH041 fibroblast cells 8 undergoing quiescence.

Figure 3. Centrosome amplification induces senescence in WI-38 fibroblast and paracrine cell protrusions.

(a-d)  Conditioned  media  from  MCF10A  cells  with  centrosome  amplification  stimulates  the leading-edge  displacement  of  wild-type  cells. (a)  Representative  images  of leading-edge displacement in  cells  with  and  without centrosome  amplification,  with  or  without  conditioned media swap. Colour code on the heatmap and images represents the position of leading edge at different time points.

(b,c)  Quantitative  analysis  of  leading-edge  displacement  in  MCF10A  cells  with  (PLK4,  b)  or without  (control,  c)  centrosome  amplification,  before  and  after  exchange  with  the  indicated conditioned medium.

(d)  Conditioned  media  from  MCF10A  cells  with  centrosome  amplification  significantly increases  the  average  protrusion  velocity  of  control  cells.  Shown  is  the  average  velocity  of leading-edge displacement in cells with and without centrosome amplification, with or without conditioned media swap.

(e,f) Increased SAβ -Gal staining in WI38 fibroblasts with centrosome amplification. Representative images (e) and quantification (f) of SAβ -Gal (blue) staining of WI38 fibroblasts with and without centrosome amplification or positive control with doxorubicin treatment. (g,h) Centrosome amplification induces Senescence-Associated Heterochromatin Foci formation in WI38 cells. Images (g) and quantification (h) of senescence-associated heterochromatin foci (Hoechst) in WI38 cells with and without centrosome amplification.

Figure 4. Centrosome amplification induces a HIF-1 α inflammatory response independent of NF κ B.

- (a) Only modest induction of pro-inflammatory cytokines and undetectable levels of GATA4 or IL6 in MCF10A cells after induction of centrosome amplification. Indicated are the mRNA fold changes of the PLK4 samples relative to the 608 control from RNAseq (left) and RT-qPCR of IL6 (right) in MCF10A cells. Data are means + s.e.m. from n=3 independent experiments, ns, not significant, ****P <0:0001; adjusted P value(grey) analyzed with DESeq2 Wald test.
- (b)  Induction  of  NFκ B  regulated  genes  after  centrosome  amplification  from  PLK4  transient overexpression,  but  not  from  cytokinesis  failure.  Shown  are  GSEA  FDR:  q  values  and normalized enrichment score(NES) for the indicated cell lines.
- (c)  CRISPR-mediated  gene  disruption  of  HIF1A  abolishes  the  induction  of  NFκ B  regulated genes  by  centrosome  amplification.  GSEA  revealed  a  statistically  insignificant  suppression  of NFκ B regulated-  genes  in  sgHIF1A  cells  with  centrosome  amplification  relative  to  sgHIF1A control cells.
- (d,e)  Lack  of  NFκ B  nuclear  accumulation  after  centrosome  amplification.  (d)  Representative images  of  p65  RELA  (NFκ B)  and  DAPI  (grey)  in  WI38  cells  with  and  without  centrosome amplification  as  compared  with  TNF α treated  cells.  (e)  Quantification  of  p65  RELA  (NFκ B) nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio in WI-38 cells with or without centrosome amplification as compared to TNF α treatment.

All data are means + s.e.m. from n=3 independent experiments, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001,****P <0.0001; analysed with Student's t-test. Scale bars, 50 μ m.

- (a,b)  Low-dose  MPS  inhibition  by  reversine,  at  concentrations  that  generate  comparable  or higher  levels  of  aneuploidy  than  centrosome  amplification  from  transient  PLK4 61 ,  does  not induce  proliferation  arrest. metadata.preprint_title: The Senescence-Associated-Secretory Phenotype constituting HIF-1α activation is independent of micronuclei induction and associated cGAS-mediated interferon response

pub_metadata.preprint_title: The Senescence-Associated-Secretory Phenotype constituting HIF-1α activation is independent of micronuclei induction and associated cGAS-mediated interferon response
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Deng, L.; Ho, C.; Picone, R.; Abderazzaq, F.; Flanagan, N.; Low, B. C.; Wu, S. K.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: The Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype (SASP), characterized by the upregulation of inflammatory cytokines, is triggered during senescence by anti-proliferation stresses, including replicative exhaustion, {gamma}-irradiation, Ras oncogene induction, and centrosome amplification. The elucidation of common signalling pathway(s) activated in SASP, induced by different anti-proliferation stresses, remains an important question. Indeed, micronuclei activation of the cGAS/Sting pathway, which has been thought to drive SASP(Kwon, Leibowitz, and Lee, 2020), remains controversial(Flynn, Koch, and Mitchison, 2021; Sato and Hayashi, 2024; Takaki et al., 2024). In this report, analyses of various cell lines induced to undergo senescence by diverse stressors revealed that HIF-1 is specifically induced in senescence but not in quiescence. Consistent with our previous findings(Wu et al., 2023a), we have further demonstrated how centrosome amplification induces a non-canonical SASP dominated by HIF-1 activation rather than the classical NF{kappa}B signaling. Lastly, we revealed that during SASP, centrosome amplification-generated micronuclei do not activate the cGAS/Sting-mediated interferon response. Together, our findings demonstrate that HIF-1-activation in SASP is a defining feature of the SASP induced by diverse stressors, acting independently of micronuclei generation and cGAS/Sting activation.
